name: Root Cause Agent Evaluation
description: Comprehensive test cases for root cause determination covering deploys, config changes, infrastructure, security, and ambiguous scenarios.

parts.text:
Investigation findings for P1 payment-service outage:

Log findings: NullPointerException in PaymentProcessor.charge() at ~200/min. First occurrence: 14:02 UTC. Affected versions: v2.14.0. Last healthy version: v2.13.0. Correlated event: Deploy v2.14.0 at 14:02 UTC.

Affected services: payment-service (down, primary), checkout-service (degraded, downstream), order-service (degraded, downstream).

Impact: ~15,000 users affected, ~$2,400/min revenue loss.

args.probable_cause: Null pointer exception in PaymentProcessor.charge() introduced in v2.14.0, likely due to missing null-check on a newly added or modified field in the payment processing path.
evidence: Error first appeared at exactly 14:02, matching deploy time of v2.14.0
Last healthy version v2.13.0 had no occurrences of this error
100% error rate immediately after deploy — not a gradual degradation
contributing_factors: No canary or staged rollout — full deployment to all instances simultaneously
Missing null-safety validation in payment processing path
No automated rollback trigger on error rate spike

parts.text: Root cause identified with 95% confidence: v2.14.0 introduced a null pointer exception in PaymentProcessor.charge(). The error onset perfectly correlates with the deploy time, and the previous version was healthy.

Investigation findings for P2 api-gateway latency:

Log findings: ConnectionPoolExhaustedException at ~80/min. First occurrence: 09:45 UTC. Affected version: v4.2.1 (same version was healthy before). Correlated event: Traffic spike from marketing campaign at 09:30 UTC.

Affected services: api-gateway (degraded, primary).

Impact: ~8,000 users experiencing timeouts, ~$800/min revenue impact.

args.probable_cause: RDS connection pool sized too small for traffic volume. Marketing campaign at 09:30 drove traffic beyond the connection pool's capacity, causing pool exhaustion 15 minutes later as connections backed up.
evidence: Traffic spike from marketing campaign began at 09:30, connection errors started at 09:45 — 15 min lag consistent with pool saturation
Same code version was healthy before the traffic spike, ruling out a code change
contributing_factors: Connection pool max size not tuned for peak traffic
No auto-scaling policy triggered by connection pool utilization
Marketing campaign not coordinated with engineering team

parts.text: Root cause identified with 85% confidence: RDS connection pool undersized for the traffic volume driven by a marketing campaign. The 15-minute lag between traffic spike and errors is consistent with gradual pool exhaustion.

Investigation findings for P3 recommendation-engine CPU:

Log findings: Slow query warnings (recommendation scoring >500ms) at ~20/min. First occurrence: 11:20 UTC. Affected version: v1.8.3. Last healthy version: v1.8.3 (same version). No correlated event.

Affected services: recommendation-engine (degraded, primary).

Impact: ~2,000 users seeing slower recommendations, no revenue impact.

args.probable_cause: Recommendation scoring algorithm's CPU usage has gradually exceeded instance capacity due to organic traffic growth, with 2 of 6 instances hitting resource limits during peak hours.
evidence: No code change or deploy correlated with the onset — same version was previously healthy
CPU increase is gradual and affecting only a subset of instances, consistent with organic growth
contributing_factors: No horizontal auto-scaling configured for recommendation-engine
Recommendation scoring algorithm not optimized for current dataset size
No capacity planning alerts before hitting the 80% threshold

parts.text: Root cause identified with 65% confidence: organic traffic growth has pushed recommendation-engine CPU beyond capacity on a subset of instances. No deploy or event triggered this — it's a capacity planning gap.

Investigation findings for P2 search-service latency:

Log findings: ElasticsearchTimeoutException: query execution exceeded 10000ms at ~60/min. First occurrence: 10:15 UTC. Affected version: v5.2.0 (same version running for 2 weeks). Correlated event: Feature flag 'enable_fuzzy_search_v2' toggled ON at 10:12 UTC via LaunchDarkly.

Affected services: search-service (degraded, primary), product-catalog-service (degraded, downstream).

Impact: ~12,000 users experiencing search timeouts, ~$400/min revenue impact.

args.probable_cause: Feature flag 'enable_fuzzy_search_v2' enabled at 10:12 UTC activated a new fuzzy search query path that generates expensive Elasticsearch queries (likely wildcard or regex patterns), causing query timeouts under production load.
evidence: Timeouts began at 10:15 UTC, 3 minutes after feature flag was toggled — consistent with query queue buildup
Same code version (v5.2.0) was healthy for 2 weeks before the flag change, ruling out a code deploy
Error pattern is specifically Elasticsearch query timeout, matching the feature flag's scope (search queries)
contributing_factors: Feature flag enabled in production without load testing the new query pattern
No gradual rollout percentage on the feature flag — toggled to 100% immediately
No query performance guardrails or timeout circuit breaker on Elasticsearch queries

parts.text: Root cause identified with 90% confidence: feature flag 'enable_fuzzy_search_v2' activated an expensive Elasticsearch query path. The timing perfectly correlates with the flag toggle, and the same code was healthy for 2 weeks prior.

Investigation findings for P1 multi-service outage:

Log findings: HealthCheckFailedException: instances in us-east-1a failing TCP health checks at continuous rate. First occurrence: 11:42 UTC. Affected versions: multiple services, all versions. No code deploy correlated. Correlated event: AWS us-east-1a connectivity degradation reported on AWS status page at 11:40 UTC.

Affected services: payment-service (degraded, primary), order-service (degraded, primary), user-profile-service (degraded, primary), api-gateway (degraded, primary).

Impact: ~30,000 users affected (~33% of traffic), ~$3,500/min revenue loss.

args.probable_cause: AWS us-east-1a availability zone network connectivity failure at 11:40 UTC caused all instances in that AZ to fail health checks. Approximately one-third of the platform's capacity was in us-east-1a, causing proportional degradation across all services.
evidence: AWS status page confirmed us-east-1a connectivity degradation at 11:40 UTC, 2 minutes before first health check failures
All affected services are in us-east-1a — no services in other AZs are impacted
Multiple services affected simultaneously with no code changes, ruling out application-level cause
contributing_factors: No multi-AZ failover automation — load balancer draining was slow to remove unhealthy instances
Approximately 33% of capacity concentrated in a single AZ without redundancy margin
No cross-AZ health monitoring to trigger early AZ evacuation

parts.text: Root cause identified with 95% confidence: AWS us-east-1a availability zone network failure. Infrastructure issue confirmed by AWS status page. All affected services were in the impacted AZ. Contributing factors include insufficient multi-AZ failover automation.

Investigation findings for P1 auth-service security breach:

Log findings: Unauthorized admin API access from IP 203.0.113.42 — bulk GET requests to /api/admin/users/export and /api/admin/users/{id}/pii. 340 requests over 22 minutes. First occurrence: 03:17 UTC. Affected version: v6.1.0 (same version, no deploy). Correlated event: Admin API token last rotated 90 days ago.

Affected services: auth-service (degraded, primary), user-data-service (degraded, downstream).

Impact: ~50,000 users whose PII may have been exposed, no direct revenue impact.

args.probable_cause: Compromised admin API token used from unauthorized IP 203.0.113.42 to bulk-export customer PII. The token had not been rotated in 90 days and had overly broad permissions (read access to all user PII endpoints).
evidence: Access originated from IP 203.0.113.42 which is not in the admin allow-list
Admin API token was last rotated 90 days ago — exceeds the 30-day rotation policy
Access pattern (bulk sequential export) is inconsistent with normal admin usage patterns
contributing_factors: Admin API token rotation policy not enforced — 90 days without rotation
No IP allow-list enforcement on admin API endpoints
Admin token had broad permissions — no scoped access for specific data types
No rate limiting on admin API bulk export endpoints

parts.text: Root cause identified with 85% confidence: compromised admin API token used for unauthorized PII export. Token was 90 days old (exceeding rotation policy), used from an unauthorized IP, with overly broad permissions and no rate limiting.

Investigation findings for P2 checkout-service errors:

Log findings: Intermittent 503 ServiceUnavailableException at ~30/min. First occurrence: 08:00 UTC. Affected version: v7.3.0 (deployed 3 days ago, was healthy until now). No clear correlated event — no deploy, no config change, no traffic spike.

Affected services: checkout-service (degraded, primary).

Impact: ~4,000 users seeing intermittent checkout failures, ~$500/min revenue impact.

args.probable_cause: Intermittent resource contention or dependency failure causing 503 errors on checkout-service. Most likely a downstream dependency (database or external payment provider) experiencing intermittent issues, as the same code version was healthy for 3 days.
evidence: Code version v7.3.0 was healthy for 3 days, making a latent code bug possible but less likely
Intermittent nature (not constant failure) suggests a resource contention or external dependency issue rather than a code defect
contributing_factors: Insufficient logging — 503 errors lack detailed root cause information in stack traces
No distributed tracing to correlate checkout failures with downstream dependency health
No circuit breaker on external payment provider calls to distinguish provider issues from internal failures

parts.text: Root cause identified with 40% confidence: likely intermittent downstream dependency failure, but evidence is insufficient for a definitive conclusion. Further investigation needed — recommend adding distributed tracing and checking payment provider status.

Investigation findings for P2 user-profile-service memory leak:

Log findings: OOMKilled — container exceeded 2Gi limit. Heap dump shows growing HashMap in UserCacheManager.loadUserProfiles(). OOMKill every ~45 min on 2 of 5 pods. First occurrence: 06:15 UTC. Affected version: v3.5.2. Last healthy: v3.5.1. Correlated event: Deploy v3.5.2 at 02:00 UTC introduced new caching logic.

Affected services: user-profile-service (degraded, primary), api-gateway (degraded, downstream).

Impact: ~10,000 users with intermittent profile loading failures, ~$200/min revenue impact.

args.probable_cause: Memory leak in UserCacheManager.loadUserProfiles() introduced in v3.5.2. New caching logic accumulates user profile objects in a HashMap without eviction, causing heap growth until OOMKill. The 4-hour delay between deploy and symptoms indicates gradual memory accumulation.
evidence: Heap dump directly shows growing HashMap in UserCacheManager.loadUserProfiles() — the exact class modified in v3.5.2
v3.5.1 had no memory issues — leak correlated with the new caching logic in v3.5.2
OOMKill frequency (~45 min) is consistent with gradual memory leak accumulation, not a sudden spike
contributing_factors: No cache size limit or eviction policy configured in the new caching implementation
Memory usage not included in deployment health check criteria — leak went undetected for 4 hours
No load testing with sustained traffic to catch gradual memory leaks before production deploy

parts.text: Root cause identified with 90% confidence: memory leak in UserCacheManager.loadUserProfiles() from v3.5.2. Heap dump confirms growing HashMap without eviction. The 4-hour delay between deploy and symptoms is consistent with gradual accumulation.
